schools.summary: The Pharisees and scribes come far from Jerusalem to find fault, not to learn; 'common' = unclean (Jerome); the charge is transgressing the elders' tradition, not the law.
Bede contrasts the seeking crowds with the cavilling teachers; Pseudo-Jerome reads the unwashed disciples as the coming fellowship of the Gentiles with the apostles.
Henry: Christ's design to set aside the man-made ceremonial law that was added to God's law, discharging the disciples from it.

Mark explains 'common' (koinos: common > profane > ceremonially unclean; cf. Acts 10:14) for Gentile readers; the objection is ceremonial not sanitary; 'they found fault' is the spies' aim, the clause textually uncertain.

Mark's note that the custom spread from the Pharisees to all the Jews; the crux 'oft' (pygmē, 'with the fist'/up to the wrist/diligently); the tradition of the elders as custom handed down, not Mosaic (Gal 1:14; Col 2:8; Matt 15:2).
Henry: the elders made an indifferent custom binding on pain of excommunication, gaining authority over consciences.

Market contact risked ceremonial defilement (exclusiveness, not hygiene); 'wash' (baptisōntai) discussed — immersion of the body, or (variant) sprinkling; the washing of cups, pots (xestēs, a Latin loan), and couches multiplied endlessly (cf. Heb 9:10).
Wesley/Clarke: the Greek baptism-word means washing or sprinkling indifferently — cups washed, couches sprinkled.

debates.question: Does the 'baptism' of vessels and couches imply immersion?

sides.position: It means washing in any manner, even sprinkling (couches could not be immersed)
The verb properly implies immersion of the body / dipping of vessels

schools.summary: The challenge is put to Christ himself to make him enforce the tradition; framed by the human rule ('according to the tradition'), making the elders the standard; the better text reads 'defiled hands' (Ellicott).
Henry: they covet to make themselves the standard; the disciples' liberty from tradition is a truer obedience, not laxity.
Mark sets the Isaiah citation (29:13) first; 'Well' (kalōs) appositely here, ironic in v.9; 'you hypocrites' the pointed charge (Matt 15:7).
Pseudo-Chrysostom: they are hypocrites revering the unworthy, honouring men's doctrines while their heart is far from God.
Henry: lip-honour governed by ambition and covetousness is worship in vain — neither pleasing to God nor profitable to them.

'Worship' here is inward devotion (Acts 18:13), pronounced empty because self-devised; 'teaching for doctrines the commandments of men' imposes human precepts as binding (Ellicott distinguishes the Greek terms for many precepts vs the one broad command).
Henry: enforcing church-canons instead of the great principles of religion, judging men by conformity to tradition, not obedience to God.

'Laying aside' = rejecting God's law for tradition held superior (the Pharisees' uniform doctrine); the sharp contrast of command vs tradition (Talmud-confirmed); the washing of pots a trivial example, the clause textually uncertain.
Henry: superstition is endless — one human rule admitted breeds many, crowding out God's weighty commands.

'Full well' (kalōs) is scathing irony, not praise; 'reject' means rescind/repeal (Gal 3:15), stronger than 'set aside' in v.8; Clarke's rabbinic quote ('observe the law, neglect the rabbins') proves the inversion Christ condemns.
The command God gave through Moses (Exod 20:12; Deut 5:16; Exod 21:17; Lev 20:9) — to honour parents, with death for cursing them.
Bede: 'honour' includes alms and support, not mere court; Henry: children must relieve poor parents — to starve them is worse than to curse them.
Cyril of Jerusalem: honour earthly parents (Eph 6:1), but obey Christ when they oppose godliness; contempt of dutiful parents falls under the curse.

themes: obey God above parents when they conflict

schools.summary: Corban (Hebrew, preserved by Mark) = a gift/offering, applied to the Temple treasury (Lev 2:1; Num 7:3; Matt 27:6); the rabbis let the mere word free a son from supporting his parents, even reserving it for his own use.
Theophylact: the Pharisees taught the device to devour the offerings themselves, deceiving sons into neglecting parents.

themes: the vow that freed from duty
religion as a cloak for greed

schools.summary: Gill on the rabbinic canon: a vow contrary to a command must be kept and the command broken; only a 'wise man' could revoke it — setting the doctors' authority above God's law.
Pseudo-Chrysostom and Theophylact: the device, made for gain, did not allow parents to be honoured.
Henry: a wicked vow held to discharge a man from God's holy law — the pattern of later corruptions like monastic vows.

themes: a vow above the fifth commandment
human authority binding what God looses

schools.summary: 'Making of none effect' = nullify/cancel (akyrountes, Gal 3:17), stronger than 'set aside' (v.9); the tradition the instrument of the cancelling, delivered on the scribes' own authority; 'many such like things' widens the charge with scorn (cf. v.8).
Theophylact: the scribes transgress God's law for gain — a vow made binding above the divine command.

themes: making the word of God void
a foolish vow above God's law

schools.summary: Christ turns to the crowd with a pointed appeal to understand and see through the scribes' hypocrisy (Acts 8:30; Matt 15:11); corrupt customs cured by rectifying corrupt notions (Henry).

Pseudo-Chrysostom: the things of Christ touch the inner man, the law's external rites soon ended by the cross; Bede: a man is clean or unclean by the state of his heart, and evil thoughts arise from his own will, not the devil as author.
Theophylact: what is in a man's power is imputed to him, proceeding from the interior will.

themes: evil thoughts from one's own will

schools.summary: Gill: nothing from without (food, washed or not) can defile the soul; the defilement that matters comes from within.
Wesley: excess in meat or drink is real guilt, but the pollution arises from the heart's wickedness, proportionable to it — the food itself innocent.
Bede/Pseudo-Chrysostom: meats reach not the soul; only an inordinate use, from a disordered mind, defiles — Christ brings in the contrary of bodily purification.

themes: the heart the seat of defilement
excess a sin of the will

schools.summary: Christ's customary close to a parable (Mark 4:9; Matt 13:9; 11:15), prompting the disciples to ask 'concerning the parable'; the verse omitted in many good MSS, perhaps a marginal note.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the baptismal anointing of the ears to hear the divine mysteries fulfils 'he that hath ears, let him hear.'

themes: he that hath ears, let him hear
a deep saying to be pondered

schools.summary: The private house (probably Peter's in Capernaum); the disciples ask the parable's meaning (cf. Mark 4:10; Peter the spokesman, Matt 15:15); Barnes: their Pharisaic notions of ceremonial defilement made it a riddle.
Pseudo-Chrysostom: they called it a parable because it was not clear to them.

The gentle rebuke 'are ye so without understanding also?' — the disciples, reared on clean/unclean, slow to grasp its abrogation; 'cannot defile him' = cannot pollute the soul or make a sinner needing ceremonial cleansing.
Theophylact: the Lord begins by chiding them before unfolding the meaning.

themes: external things cannot pollute the soul

schools.summary: Food reaches the belly, not the heart (the mind, Hebrew idiom; Prov 9:4; 1 Cor 6:13); 'purging all meats' (better text: masculine participle) is Mark's comment that Jesus thereby declared all foods clean — anticipating Acts 10:15 (cf. 10:14), a revolutionary abolition of the clean/unclean distinction.

sides.position: Mark's comment that Jesus (the speaker) thereby made all foods clean (cf. Acts 10:15)
The natural process — the draught carries off the impure, leaving the nourishing

schools.summary: Barnes: what comes out — words and conduct expressing inward malice, lust, etc. — defiles, rendering the soul corrupt before God (cf. Matt 15:18).
Henry: a corrupt heart, like a corrupt fountain, sends forth corrupt streams; our care must be to wash the heart from wickedness.
Gloss: evil thoughts belong to the heart/mind, by which a man is called clean or unclean.

The heart the desperately wicked source (Jer 17:9; Gen 6:5); 'evil thoughts' first as the spring of all; the police-court catalogue of fornications, thefts, murders proceeding from within (Matt 15:19).
Henry: ill-thinking put first as the spring of all our commissions, the corrupt fountain issuing in corrupt deeds.

themes: the native produce of the soul

schools.summary: The vices defined: covetousness/wickedness plural (manifold); deceit a baited snare; the 'evil eye' = malignant envy (Matt 20:15); pride = haughtiness (rare; Rom 1:30); foolishness = the infatuation of impiety (Prov 14:18); Mark omits false witness and adds several others.
Wesley: wickedness = ill-nature and cruelty; foolishness = wild imaginations contrary to sobriety.
Henry: ill-thinking first, unthinking foolishness last — the spring of our omissions; the list's variety proves the fountain deeply corrupt.

Gill: all evils rise from within, not from imitation (example draws out but does not produce); hence the inward part is corrupt before the act — the fruit of original sin derived from Adam (Job 14:4).
Henry: to defile is to render unfit for communion with God and, unmortified, to shut men out of the new Jerusalem — the cleansing needed is of the heart, not the hands.
The second withdrawal from Galilee into heathen Tyre and Sidon (Matt 15:21), seeking privacy and rest; 'could not be hid' — his fame reaches even there.
Pseudo-Augustine: the time to preach to Gentiles had not come, yet he would not grudge faith to those who sought him; Pseudo-Jerome reads the woman as the Church of the nations.

Calvin: as the Father's ambassador he kept within his calling, so that 'what he wished he was unable'; the episode condemns the Jews — the covenant-heirs deaf while a stranger comes of her own accord.

themes: sought privacy, could not be hid
the door opened to the Gentiles
the woman as the Gentile Church

schools.summary: Christ's fame had reached heathen Tyre and Sidon (Luke 6:17); the tender diminutive 'little daughter' possessed by an unclean spirit; the mother comes at once.
Henry: falling at his feet is the posture of all true suppliants; Christ never put away any who fell at his feet.

'Greek' = Gentile (Jews so called all non-Jews, Rom 1:16); 'Syrophenician' a Latin form (Mark's Latinisms) — Greek in religion, Syrian in tongue, Phoenician in race; 'besought' an imperfect (she kept at it).
Henry: the greatest blessing to ask for our children is that Christ break Satan's power in their souls; her Gentile faith shines against Israel's unbelief.

themes: the best plea for a child

schools.summary: The Jews ('children') have the first claim (Matt 10:5; Rom 2:9); the Gentiles called 'dogs' as the Jews reckoned them; 'first' implies mercy in reserve for the Gentiles, not far off (Henry).
Theophylact/Pseudo-Chrysostom: the delay is no deficiency of power but a trial of her firm faith, teaching us not to grow weary in prayer; her faith lays bare Jewish unbelief.

themes: mercy in reserve for the Gentiles

schools.summary: Her humble, witty reply turns Christ's word to her plea (a unique combination of faith and wit); the diminutives — little dogs, little crumbs, little children — heighten the tender picture.
Pseudo-Chrysostom/Theophylact: placing herself among the dogs is reverence; she asks only the crumbs, a small portion of the children's abundance.

'For this saying' = for the faith it revealed (not his virtue named); her bright repartee pleased Jesus; Mark omits 'great is thy faith' and states the grant more definitely; the word effects the cure.
Henry: the scene encourages persevering, importunate prayer that prevails at last.

She goes home believing and finds it so; Mark's graphic detail of the daughter laid quietly on the bed (Ethiopic: 'clothed, sitting'); the perfect 'gone out' marks the demon gone for good (cf. 1 John 3:8).
Henry: Christ conquers Satan at a distance — the Spirit of the Lord is not bound; faith took him at his word and was not disappointed.
The route back from Tyre and Sidon through the region of Decapolis (ten cities east of Jordan) to the sea of Galilee; Bede maps the geography precisely.
Theophylact: Christ returns lest the Jews charge him with communing with Gentiles; the healing fitly follows a deliverance from a devil.
Pseudo-Jerome allegorises the route — Tyre = Judaea's narrowness, Sidon = hunting, the sea = inconstancy, Decapolis = the Decalogue — figuring the deaf and dumb human race brought to Christ.

themes: the deaf race brought to Christ

schools.summary: Deaf with an impediment (mogilalos, a stammerer, also 'dumb'; the deafness likely the cause), the word echoing Isaiah 35:6; his friends bring him and beg the laying on of hands; the incident only in Mark.
Calvin: the laying on of hands was a known symbol of consecration and gift-bestowing, so they ask only what Christ was wont to do.
Taking aside to shun ostentation and to reach the deaf man's attention/faith by touch (Ellicott); the fingers and spittle a sign that the healing power came from Jesus (Mark 8:23; John 9:6).
Pseudo-Chrysostom/Theophylact: the touch shows his body, united to Deity, consecrated by divine power, restoring human nature marred in Adam.
Calvin: no need of allegory — the spittle on the tongue and fingers in the ears show that speech and hearing come from Christ alone.
The upward look an attitude of prayer for the man's sake (John 11:41); the sigh the sadness of sympathy (John 11:33); Ephphatha the very Aramaic word preserved by Mark.
Bede: as man he looks up and groans, as God he heals with one word — the two natures distinct in the one Christ; Pseudo-Chrysostom: he groans pitying fallen human nature.
Wesley: 'Be opened' is a word of sovereign authority, not a prayer for power, for Christ had a perpetual fund of power in himself (John 5:21).

debates.question: Was the upward look and sigh a prayer for power, or sympathy and example?

sides.position: Sympathy and example for the man's sake; he healed by his own authority
A genuine attitude of prayer and strong feeling, the vehemence of his love

themes: two natures in the one Christ

schools.summary: The cure immediate and complete — both ears opened, the 'bond' of the tongue loosed (not anatomical; cf. Luke 13:16), he began to speak correctly (orthōs, inchoative); the visible fulfilment of Isaiah 35:5-6.
The charge of silence (the Messianic secret, lest the Pharisees' jealousy be roused); the double comparative shows the more he forbade, the more they published — prohibitions provoke (Robertson).
Calvin: not a covert bidding to spread it, but a real intent to delay; their zeal was unseasonable, yet Christ overruled it to his glory, leaving the district inexcusable.

themes: the charge to tell no man
the more forbidden, the more published

schools.summary: Unbounded amazement ('very abundantly'); 'He hath done all things well' = perfectly effected the cure; the words echo Isaiah 35:5-6 and the Creator's prerogative (Exod 4:11); Ellicott contrasts Mark's precise diagnosis with popular amazement.
Clarke: 'all things well' is true of all Christ's conduct — in creation, providence, and redemption alike, all done well.

themes: He hath done all things well
the deaf hear, the dumb speak
